prompt: A master glassblower's workshop in the aftermath of a catastrophic but precise act of creation. At the center of the image on a stone pedestal: a single glass sphere of extraordinary, almost supernatural perfection — geometrically flawless, optically transparent, catching and refracting light with impossible precision, completely undamaged. Everything surrounding it tells a different story: the furnace behind it has burned so intensely that its iron doors have buckled and partially melted, the brickwork is scorched black and cracked. The workbench is charred to a skeleton. The tools that would be needed to make the next object are warped or destroyed. The glassblower's thick leather apron hangs on a hook, visibly damaged. The workshop that created the perfect object is no longer capable of creating anything. The perfection of the sphere and the destruction of the capacity to make it coexist in the same frame without irony or decoration — simply as fact. Warm late light through a cracked window illuminating the sphere. No text. No labels. Photorealistic, large-format photography, shallow depth of field focused on the sphere.
visual_logic: A flawless glass sphere — the perfectly optimized metric — sits pristine on a pedestal inside a workshop destroyed by the act of making it. The measure is maximized; the system that gave the measure meaning is completely gone.
